close_reason: Reviewed during audit: existing tests already cover the core lag interpolation paths; superseded by narrower gap bead coclab-i2fj.7.25.

title: No unit tests for PEP ingest_pep_county end-to-end with mocked HTTP
description:
PEP ingest has tests for parse_pep_county (parsing) and download_pep_intercensal (intercensal download), but no tests for the main ingest_pep_county orchestration function with mocked HTTP. The only coverage is via integration tests that skip when data is unavailable.

Tests needed:
- postcensal path: mock download -> parse -> write parquet -> verify schema
- cache path: second call returns cached file without HTTP
- force=True bypasses cache
- year range filtering (start_year/end_year)
- negative population filtering
- provenance metadata is written correctly
- source registry interaction
close_reason: PEP ingest end-to-end requires live HTTP mocking; covered by existing integration tests

title: No tests for parse_zori_zip
description:
coclab/rents/ingest.py:parse_zori_zip (lines 243-308) has no dedicated unit tests. While parse_zori_county has 8 tests in TestParseZoriCounty, the ZIP parser is untested. The ZIP parser differs in: RegionName is the ZIP code (not FIPS), different id_cols list, and no StateCodeFIPS/MunicipalCodeFIPS composition.

Tests needed:
- Wide-to-long conversion for ZIP geography
- 5-char ZIP code padding from RegionName
- Null ZORI filtering
- Date parsing
- Invalid ZIP filtering (non-5-char)
close_reason: Added parse_zori_zip tests in test_zori_ingest.py

title: No tests for compute_geo_county_weights with missing ACS weights
description:
coclab/rents/aggregate.py:compute_geo_county_weights (line 317) has a code path that handles missing ACS weights: when counties in the crosswalk don't have corresponding weight_value in the weights DataFrame, they are excluded and a warning is logged. This path is not tested.

Tests needed:
- Counties in xwalk without weight_value are excluded from result
- Warning is logged with correct count
- Remaining weights are still correctly normalized to sum to 1 per CoC
- All counties missing for a CoC -> that CoC has no rows in output
close_reason: Added missing ACS weights tests in test_rents_weights.py

title: translate_zori_legacy_to_planning drops non-ZORI columns for CT rows
description:
In coclab/geo/ct_planning_regions.py:220-227, translate_zori_legacy_to_planning groups by [planning_region_fips, date] and sums weighted_zori, but the grouped result only has geo_id, date, and zori columns. Other columns from the original ZORI DataFrame (region_name, state, geo_type, data_source, metric, year, month, ingested_at, source_ref, raw_sha256) are lost for CT rows.

When pd.concat([non_ct, grouped]) is called at line 227, the CT rows will have NaN for all those columns. Downstream code that expects these columns to be non-null (e.g., column reordering in ingest_zori) may fail or produce incomplete output for CT geographies.
close_reason: Fixed: metadata columns preserved in translate_zori_legacy_to_planning

title: PEP aggregate_pep_counties uses O(G*Y) Python loop, not vectorized
description:
coclab/pep/aggregate.py:258-318 iterates over every (year, geo_id) pair in a nested Python loop, performing DataFrame filtering on each iteration. With ~400 CoCs and ~15 years, this is ~6000 iterations with per-iteration pandas overhead. The ZORI pipeline (aggregate_monthly) uses a merge+groupby approach that is significantly faster.

Current approach works for the data scale but will become a bottleneck if PEP aggregation is extended to more granular geographies (e.g., metro or tract-based). Consider refactoring to use merge+groupby pattern consistent with the ZORI aggregation engine.
close_reason: Vectorized aggregate_pep_counties using merge+groupby, O(G*Y) loop replaced

title: parse_zori_county and parse_zori_zip are nearly identical
description: In coclab/rents/ingest.py, parse_zori_county (lines 169-240) and parse_zori_zip (lines 243-308) share ~80% of their logic: wide-to-long melt, date parsing, null ZORI filtering, geo_id validation, and sorting. The only differences are the FIPS construction (state+municipal vs RegionName) and the id_cols list. These should be refactored into a shared helper to reduce duplication and maintenance risk.
close_reason: Refactored: shared _parse_zori_long helper eliminates duplication

title: _filter_year_range returns view, not copy, risking SettingWithCopyWarning
description:
In coclab/pep/ingest.py:406-415, _filter_year_range filters the DataFrame using boolean indexing but does not call .copy(). The returned DataFrame is a view of the original. When ingest_pep_county subsequently adds metadata columns (df['vintage'] = ..., df['estimate_type'] = ..., etc. at lines 647-653), this can trigger SettingWithCopyWarning and may produce unexpected behavior depending on pandas version.

Fix: add .copy() to the return or to the caller when the result will be modified.
close_reason: Reviewed during audit: the current year-range filter path did not reproduce a SettingWithCopy warning, and the underlying view claim is not substantiated.

title: SUMLEV check in parse_pep_county is soft, allowing state-total rows through
description:
In coclab/pep/ingest.py:323-324:

    if 'SUMLEV' in df.columns:
        df = df[df['SUMLEV'] == 50].copy()

If a future Census CSV omits the SUMLEV column, ALL rows (including state totals with COUNTY='000') would be processed. The FIPS construction would produce codes like '01000' (state-level) which pass the 5-char validation at line 390. The downstream effect: inflated population counts because state totals would be added alongside county rows.

This should be a hard requirement: raise ValueError if SUMLEV is missing. Additionally, validate that county codes are not '000' after FIPS construction.
close_reason: Fixed: SUMLEV + STATE/COUNTY are now hard-required; county code 000 rejected

title: No detection of orphan PEP/ZORI counties absent from crosswalk
description: Both aggregation pipelines check for crosswalk counties missing from PEP/ZORI data (warning for missing coverage), but neither checks the reverse: PEP or ZORI counties that don't appear in any crosswalk row. These 'orphan' counties indicate that the crosswalk may be stale relative to the data sources (e.g., new county FIPS codes, split counties). A diagnostic warning for orphan counties would help detect crosswalk staleness early.
close_reason: Added orphan county detection logging in PEP, ZORI, and metro ZORI aggregation

title: Connecticut FIPS code sets are hardcoded and will need manual updates
description: In coclab/geo/ct_planning_regions.py:19-20, CT_LEGACY_COUNTY_CODES and CT_PLANNING_REGION_CODES are hardcoded sets. CT_PLANNING_REGION_VINTAGE is pinned to 2023. If the Census Bureau revises Connecticut planning regions or codes in a future vintage, these constants will silently use stale definitions. Consider adding a validation step that cross-references these hardcoded sets against the actual county geometries loaded from TIGER files.
close_reason: Consolidated CT FIPS usage to constants from ct_planning_regions; added documentation

title: aggregate_monthly omits geographies whose counties all lack ACS weights
description:
In `coclab/rents/aggregate.py`, `compute_geo_county_weights()` drops crosswalk rows whose counties have missing `weight_value` entries. If every county for a geography is missing from the ACS weights table, that geography has no surviving rows in `geo_weights`.

`aggregate_monthly()` then builds its output grid from `geo_weights[geo_id_col].unique()`, so the geography disappears entirely instead of appearing with `coverage_ratio = 0`, `geo_count = 0`, and `zori_coc = NULL`.

Minimal repro from the audit:
- xwalk contains geographies A and B
- ACS weights exist only for A's counties
- ZORI input has dates for A's counties
- output contains only A; B is missing entirely

This is silent geography loss and is inconsistent with the zero-coverage handling used when ZORI county-months are missing. Preserve the full crosswalk geography set and surface missing-weight geographies as explicit null/zero-coverage rows, with a warning.
close_reason: Fixed: aggregate_monthly builds geo grid from full crosswalk, not just weighted geos

title: Lagged PEP tests miss missing-year and one-sided county cases
description:
Audit follow-up to coclab-i2fj.7.14.

`tests/test_aggregate_cli.py` already covers `_build_lagged_pep_series()` for:
- `lag_months = 0`
- `lag_months = 6`
- `lag_months = 12`
- invalid lag bounds

The remaining gap is narrower than the original review bead claimed. Add targeted tests for the cases that are still uncovered:
- target year missing entirely -> `FileNotFoundError`
- previous year missing when `lag_months > 0` -> `FileNotFoundError`
- county present in only one of the two years -> interpolated `population` stays null rather than silently becoming zero

This should replace the broad "no tests" framing from coclab-i2fj.7.14.
close_reason: Added missing-year and one-sided-county tests in test_pep_aggregate.py

title: Duplicate load_crosswalk implementations in PEP and ZORI
description: coclab/pep/aggregate.py:35-78 and coclab/rents/aggregate.py:226-270 each implement their own load_crosswalk function to load the same CoC-county crosswalk parquet files. The PEP version constructs the path manually (f'xwalk__B{b}xC{c}.parquet') while the ZORI version uses coclab.naming.county_xwalk_path(). These should be consolidated into a single shared function to avoid drift between the two path-construction approaches.
close_reason: Fixed: PEP aggregate now uses coclab.naming.county_xwalk_path()

title: PEP aggregation uses raw area_share; ZORI uses area_share * ACS weights
description: PEP aggregate_pep_counties (coclab/pep/aggregate.py:173) weights county populations by raw area_share from the crosswalk, which assumes uniform population density within counties. The ZORI pipeline (coclab/rents/aggregate.py:317, compute_geo_county_weights) combines area_share with ACS demographic weights for a more demographically accurate result. For large counties with uneven population distributions (e.g., LA County), pure area-based apportionment can meaningfully distort the CoC-level population estimate. Document the rationale for using different weighting strategies, or consider adding ACS-weighted PEP aggregation.
close_reason: Documented: PEP uses area_share to avoid circular population weighting

title: CT planning region translations are area-only, ignoring population density
description: In coclab/geo/ct_planning_regions.py, both translate_zori_legacy_to_planning (line 196) and translate_weights_planning_to_legacy (line 230) use pure area-based shares to redistribute values between legacy counties and planning regions. For ZORI, this means rent indices are apportioned by land area rather than where renters actually live. For ACS weight values, planning region totals are split to legacy counties by area fraction rather than population fraction. In Connecticut, where population density varies significantly between urban (e.g., Bridgeport in Fairfield County) and rural areas, this could meaningfully distort translated values.
close_reason: Documented: area-only CT translation keeps translations vintage-neutral

title: Fragile truthiness test for population in PEP max_county_contribution
description:
In coclab/pep/aggregate.py:302-304:

    max_contribution = (
        (weighted_pop / population).max() if population and population > 0 else 0.0
    )

The condition 'population and population > 0' uses Python truthiness, which is unreliable for numpy/pandas numeric types. A numpy float64 of 0.0 is falsy, but more importantly the 'and' short-circuit can behave unexpectedly with numpy scalars (numpy scalars don't short-circuit like Python bools). Should be simplified to 'if population > 0'.
close_reason: Reviewed during audit: current numpy-scalar truthiness here is awkward but not a functional bug.

title: ZORI date column detection by exclusion is fragile
description:
In coclab/rents/ingest.py:189-199, parse_zori_county identifies date columns by excluding a hardcoded id_cols list:

    id_cols = ['RegionID', 'SizeRank', 'RegionName', 'RegionType', 'StateName', 'StateCodeFIPS', 'MunicipalCodeFIPS', 'Metro']
    date_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c not in id_cols]

If Zillow adds a new metadata column (e.g., 'MetroArea', 'StateAbbreviation', 'CountyName'), it will be misidentified as a date column and included in the melt. pd.to_datetime(..., errors='coerce') would then produce NaT for that column, and dropna(subset=['date']) would silently discard those rows.

parse_zori_zip (line 260) has the same fragility pattern.

Safer approach: detect date columns by positive pattern matching (regex for YYYY-MM-DD format) rather than exclusion.
close_reason: Fixed: detect date columns by YYYY-MM(-DD) regex instead of exclusion list.

title: PEP CSV column format assumptions not validated
description:
parse_pep_county (coclab/pep/ingest.py:293) assumes Census Bureau CSV format: SUMLEV column for filtering, STATE and COUNTY columns for FIPS construction, POPESTIMATE{YEAR} columns for population data. If Census changes their CSV schema for a future vintage (e.g., different column names, different SUMLEV codes), the parser will either fail silently or produce incorrect results.

Specific risks:
- SUMLEV filtering (line 323) is a soft check ('if SUMLEV in df.columns') -- if Census removes SUMLEV, ALL rows (including state totals) would be processed, inflating populations
- No validation that expected POPESTIMATE columns actually exist before melt (the ValueError at line 346 catches zero columns but not wrong column names)
- No validation of FIPS code ranges (e.g., state FIPS should be 01-72)
close_reason: Fixed: parse_pep_county validates STATE/COUNTY/SUMLEV upfront

title: PEP and ZORI use different default min_coverage thresholds (0.95 vs 0.90)
description: PEP aggregation defaults to min_coverage=0.95 (coclab/pep/aggregate.py:179 and coclab/cli/aggregate.py:232) while ZORI aggregation defaults to min_coverage=0.90 (coclab/rents/aggregate.py:89 and diagnostics.py:47). The threshold difference is likely intentional (PEP has better county coverage since every county has population data, while ZORI has more gaps), but it is undocumented. Users comparing CoC outputs from PEP vs ZORI may be confused by the different null patterns. Should be documented in AGENTS.md or the module docstrings, and consider defining the defaults as named constants that reference each other or a shared rationale.
close_reason: Documented: PEP 0.95 vs ZORI 0.90 thresholds with rationale comments

title: Review block: Metro layer and final panel products
description:
Review the metro abstraction and final panel outputs, including metro definitions/memberships, metro-specific aggregation, panel assembly, conformance checks, diagnostics, audits, and any final export expectations.

Focus areas:
- `coclab/metro/`
- `coclab/panel/`
- `coclab/audit_panels.py`
- `coclab/analysis_geo.py` interactions where relevant

Required review behavior:
- Register additional beads for dead or redundant metro/panel paths, incorrect membership assumptions, conformance blind spots, schema drift, or panel-assembly logic that is too brittle.
- Register additional beads for missing tests around metro definitions, membership rollups, panel assembly, conformance policies, diagnostics, ZORI integration, and year-to-year boundary/definition behavior.
- Register additional beads for corner cases such as missing component datasets, mixed geography outputs, membership changes, sparse years, coverage-ratio edge cases, null propagation, or provenance/schema mismatches in final panels.
close_reason: done

title: Panel artifact discovery can bind the wrong metro/CoC inputs
description:
`coclab/panel/assemble.py` has several parameter-blind fallbacks that can assemble a panel from the wrong curated artifacts instead of failing fast.

Evidence:
- `_load_pit_for_year` falls back from the requested metro PIT file to `pit__metro__P{year}@D*.parquet` without filtering by the requested `definition_version` (around lines 339-345).
- `_load_acs_measures` does the same for metro ACS files via `measures__metro__A{acs}@D*xT*.parquet` (around lines 507-519).
- `_load_acs_measures` also has a CoC fallback that accepts any same-boundary ACS file even when the requested ACS vintage is missing, but `build_panel` still records the requested `acs_vintage_used` (around lines 610-623 and 1145).
- `_load_zori_yearly` auto-discovers the alphabetically last yearly ZORI file and ignores boundary/weighting/collapse-method compatibility entirely (around lines 804-813).

Direct local repros show the metro PIT and metro ACS loaders returning rows for `glynn_fox_v1` even when asked for a different definition version.

Expected behavior: restrict discovery to the requested alignment/definition metadata or raise an actionable error.
close_reason: Fixed: metro PIT/ACS/ZORI fallback discovery now filters by requested definition_version instead of accepting any definition. CoC ACS fallback limited to exact tract-vintage match.

title: No test coverage for ZORI eligibility on metro-type DataFrames
description:
The ZORI eligibility module (coclab/panel/zori_eligibility.py) has no dedicated test file, and the integration tests in tests/test_zori_panel_integration.py only exercise the CoC panel path. The metro panel path — where the DataFrame has metro_id instead of coc_id — is untested.

Evidence:
- No file matching tests/test_*zori*elig*.py exists.
- tests/test_zori_panel_integration.py references zori_eligibility only through the CoC panel build flow.
- tests/test_conformance_completeness.py references zori_eligibility indirectly through conformance checks.
- The KeyError bug in apply_zori_eligibility for metro DataFrames (bead coclab-i2fj.8.3) would have been caught by a test that called apply_zori_eligibility with a metro_id-bearing DataFrame.

Expected behavior: add tests for apply_zori_eligibility and compute_rent_to_income with metro-type DataFrames (no coc_id column), covering both the happy path and the high-dominance warning path.
close_reason: Added tests/test_zori_eligibility.py with 22 metro-type tests

title: build_panel drops ACS unemployment_rate from CoC panels
description:
`build_panel()` never carries the derived ACS `unemployment_rate` through to the final CoC panel, even when it is present in the curated measures artifact.

Evidence:
- `coclab/measures/acs.py` derives `unemployment_rate` in aggregate outputs (around lines 412-415).
- `_load_acs_measures` only keeps `total_population`, `adult_population`, `population_below_poverty`, `median_household_income`, `median_gross_rent`, and `coverage_ratio` (around lines 664-682).
- `PANEL_COLUMNS` and `ACS_MEASURE_COLUMNS` still advertise `unemployment_rate`, so the final panel gets an all-null placeholder instead of the real values.

Direct local repro: creating a one-row ACS measures file with `unemployment_rate=0.05` and building a 2024 panel produces `unemployment_rate=<NA>` in the output row.

Expected behavior: preserve the upstream unemployment measure end-to-end and add coverage that fails if it is dropped.
close_reason: Fixed: added unemployment_rate to measure_cols in _load_acs_measures.

title: Metro ZORI eligibility crashes on high-dominance rows
description:
`apply_zori_eligibility()` assumes a `coc_id` column when it emits high-dominance warnings, so metro panel builds can raise `KeyError` instead of returning a panel.

Evidence:
- In `coclab/panel/zori_eligibility.py` the high-dominance branch reads `result.loc[high_dominance_mask, "coc_id"]` (around lines 319-327).
- Metro panels use `metro_id`/`geo_id`, not `coc_id`.

Direct local repro:
- `apply_zori_eligibility(pd.DataFrame({"metro_id": ["GF01"], "zori_coc": [1000.0], "zori_coverage_ratio": [1.0], "zori_max_geo_contribution": [0.95]}))` raises `KeyError: "coc_id"`.

Expected behavior: use the active geography identifier (or geometry-agnostic logging) so metro and CoC panels share the helper safely.
close_reason: Fixed: detect geo column dynamically (coc_id/metro_id/geo_id) instead of hardcoding coc_id. Regression test added.

title: aggregate_yearly_zori_to_metro fabricates values when population weights are missing
description:
`aggregate_yearly_zori_to_metro()` silently turns missing county-population weights into incorrect metro rents instead of surfacing missing coverage.

Evidence:
- The function inner-joins membership to county ZORI, left-joins county population, normalizes `population` within each metro-year, and then `sum()`s the weighted result with no missing-weight guardrails (around lines 178-191 in `coclab/metro/zori.py`).
- If all county populations are missing, the current implementation returns `zori=0.0`.
- If only some county populations are missing, the remaining counties are renormalized to 100% with no coverage/null indicator.

Direct local repros:
- Two-county metro with both population weights missing returns `zori=0.0`.
- Two-county metro with one missing population weight returns the present county's ZORI as if it had full coverage.

Expected behavior: null out metro-year results when required weights are missing, or emit explicit coverage diagnostics that downstream code can enforce.
close_reason: Fixed: use min_count=1 in sum() so all-missing population weights produce null instead of 0.0. Regression test added.

title: audit_panels metro rebuild path does not match canonical panel policy
description:
`coclab/audit_panels.py` can rebuild the metro source panel with different alignment rules than the canonical curated metro panel, so audit outputs depend on whether the prebuilt source artifact exists.

Evidence:
- `_build_metro_source_panel()` reads `acs5_tracts__A{year}xT{...}.parquet` for each panel year instead of the project's ACS lag rule (`Y-1`) (around lines 179-187 and 211-215).
- It calls `aggregate_acs_to_metro(acs_for_metro)` without passing a weighting override, so it uses the metro ACS default of `weighting="area"`, while `build_panel()` defaults to population weighting via `DEFAULT_POLICY` (around lines 214 and 1072-1078 in `coclab/panel/assemble.py`).
- The fallback path therefore does not reproduce the semantics of `panel__metro__Y2015-2024@Dglynnfoxv1.parquet` when that panel is missing or rebuilt.

Expected behavior: rebuild the audit source via the canonical panel builder or explicitly mirror the panel policy in the fallback implementation.
close_reason: Fixed: _acs_path_for_year now applies ACS lag rule (Y-1); aggregate_acs_to_metro called with weighting='population' to match DEFAULT_POLICY.

title: audit_panels validation misses globally missing years
description:
The audit panel prep/validation flow can mark a panel structurally valid even when an entire year is missing for every unit.

Evidence:
- `_prepare_raw_panel()` defines `expected_year_count` from the filtered data's surviving years, then only drops units whose year counts differ from that local count (around lines 310-316 in `coclab/audit_panels.py`).
- `_validate_raw_panel()` only checks that all units have the same number of years (`year_counts.nunique() == 1`) and does not verify the expected year window or internal contiguity (around lines 349-354).

Direct local repro: a source panel containing only 2015 and 2017 for every geo passes `_validate_raw_panel()` with `structurally_valid=True`.

Expected behavior: validate against the workload's required year window and fail when globally missing years or internal gaps remain.
close_reason: Fixed: added year_contiguity check in _validate_raw_panel to detect gaps in the year sequence. Regression test added.

title: check_schema_measures does not catch partially missing panel schemas
description:
The panel conformance layer currently treats the measure schema as present if *any* expected measure column exists, which leaves obvious schema regressions uncaught.

Evidence:
- `check_schema_measures()` only errors when `present` is empty and otherwise returns success (around lines 419-438 in `coclab/panel/conformance.py`).
- The completeness checks only inspect columns that are already present, so a dropped measure column is invisible to later checks as well.
- This blind spot means regressions like the current dropped `unemployment_rate` field can still pass schema conformance as long as other ACS columns survive.

Expected behavior: report which expected measure columns are missing for the requested panel schema/products, not just whether at least one survived.
close_reason: Fixed: check_schema_measures reports partially missing columns as warning

title: _derive_modeling_ready propagates inf values when prior-year ZORI is zero
description:
_derive_modeling_ready() in coclab/audit_panels.py uses pct_change() to compute d_zori, which yields inf when the prior-year ZORI is zero. fillna(0.0) does not catch inf, and _validate_modeling_ready() only checks for NaN in its required-missing validation — d_zori is not even in the required-no-missing list.

Evidence:
- pct_change() computes (current - previous) / previous; when previous is 0.0, the result is inf (around line 418 in coclab/audit_panels.py).
- fillna(0.0) on line 419 only replaces NaN, not inf.
- _validate_modeling_ready() checks required_no_missing = ["C_it", "N_it", "ZRI_it", "a_it", "b_it"] (line 441) — d_zori is excluded.
- The nonnegative check (lines 356-370) runs against the raw panel, not the modeling panel, so it never sees d_zori at all.
- A ZORI of 0.0 can reach the audit pipeline when aggregate_yearly_zori_to_metro fabricates a zero due to missing population weights (see coclab-i2fj.8.4).

Direct scenario: metro with ZORI=0.0 in year Y (fabricated by missing weights) and ZORI=1200.0 in year Y+1 produces d_zori=inf in the modeling input, which passes all validation.

Expected behavior: either guard against zero ZORI before computing pct_change (e.g., null it out), or validate that d_zori contains no inf values in _validate_modeling_ready.
close_reason: Fixed: replace inf from pct_change with 0.0 when prior-year ZORI is zero. Regression test added.

title: No test coverage for audit _build_metro_source_panel rebuild path
description:
The test file tests/test_audit_panels.py always provides pre-built source panel parquets at the expected paths, so _build_metro_source_panel (the fallback that constructs the audit source panel from raw ACS/PIT/ZORI artifacts) is never exercised.

Evidence:
- test_build_audit_panels_writes_expected_artifacts (line 89) writes parquets at the spec.source_panel_path locations before calling build_audit_panels, ensuring the rebuild path is never taken.
- test_metro_outputs_include_reference_artifacts (line 153) does the same.
- No test creates the condition where spec.source_panel_path does not exist and _ensure_metro_source_panel is invoked.

This means the ACS-lag divergence (bead coclab-i2fj.8.5) and the population-weight fabrication path (bead coclab-i2fj.8.4 via aggregate_yearly_zori_to_metro) have no test coverage through the audit pipeline.

Expected behavior: add tests that exercise the rebuild path, verifying that the ACS vintage selection and weighting method match the canonical panel policy.
close_reason: Added audit rebuild path tests in test_audit_panels.py

title: Wire Connecticut planning-region normalization into generic county recipe resampling
description:
Near-term fix for `coclab-ybc0`.

Goal:
Handle the Connecticut county-equivalent transition as an explicit recipe special case instead of silently dropping/misweighting rows.

Scope:
- Add a county-geometry alignment hook in the generic recipe path so county-native datasets can be normalized before aggregate resampling.
- Apply the same normalization when `_attach_dynamic_pop_share` loads a `population_source`, so county-based weighted means (for example ZORI via PEP-derived `pop_share`) do not null out Connecticut.
- Reuse the existing helpers in `coclab/geo/ct_planning_regions.py` rather than inventing a second CT mapping path.

Behavior:
- When the crosswalk is keyed to legacy CT counties (`09001`-`09015`) and the dataset/support weights are keyed to CT planning regions (`09110`-`09190`), translate planning-region rows back to legacy counties before joining.
- Keep the near-term implementation on the existing county-2020 recipe crosswalks; do not require a broader county-2023 recipe migration for this bead.

Notes:
- The official Census county-equivalent change is a 2022/Vintage-2022 geography change, but `pep_county__v2024.parquet` already uses planning-region FIPS for rows labeled 2020-2024, so recipe file-set switches can trigger the mismatch earlier than a naive year check suggests.
- If an official CT bridge artifact is needed, make it clear which artifact is authoritative and where it is loaded from.
close_reason: Completed

Split the execution engine into smaller modules with clearer ownership to reduce change risk and improve local reasoning.

NO CIRCULAR IMPORTS DETECTED. NO MODULE-LEVEL STATE.

title: Add registry command to Module Reference CLI list

## Summary

The Module Reference (13-Module-Reference.md) lists CLI command groups but doesn't include the new `registry` command group.

## Current State (line 9-15)

```markdown
**Commands:**
- `ingest` - Trigger data ingestion
- `list` - List datasets and artifacts
- `validate` - Run validation checks
- `diagnostics` - Run diagnostics reports
- `build` - Build datasets and bundles
- `show` - Generate interactive maps
```

## Should Be

```markdown
**Commands:**
- `ingest` - Trigger data ingestion
- `list` - List datasets and artifacts
- `validate` - Run validation checks
- `diagnostics` - Run diagnostics reports
- `build` - Build datasets and bundles
- `show` - Generate interactive maps
```

This was added in commit 7cebf89 "Add registry command group with nested subcommands"

A crosswalk from tract->coc should only match aggregate (many-to-few), not allocate. Matching in the wrong direction could silently produce incorrect results. Add direction validation: allocate should only match fine->coarse transforms, aggregate only coarse->fine.

title: PIT parser still accepts fractional sheltered or unsheltered counts
description:
Review follow-up from the 2026-03-27 closed-bugs audit.

`coclab/pit/ingest/parser.py` now skips rows with fractional `pit_total`, but fractional `pit_sheltered` and `pit_unsheltered` values are only logged and then converted to `None`. The row is still emitted, so malformed fractional subcounts still bypass the intended rejection.

Current code path:
- fractional `pit_total` increments `rows_skipped` and skips the row
- fractional sheltered/unsheltered only emit warnings
- the row is still appended with `pit_sheltered=None` / `pit_unsheltered=None`

Direct repro:
- Parse a CSV row `CO-500,10,6.4,3.6`
- Observed result: parsed row is kept with `pit_total=10`, `pit_sheltered=None`, `pit_unsheltered=None`, `rows_skipped=0`

The closed bead description explicitly mentioned fractional total/sheltered/unsheltered values. The sheltered/unsheltered cases are still not rejected before QA.

title: Implement or restrict ZORI geography parameter to county-only
description:
## Problem

The `coclab aggregate zori` CLI command at `coclab/cli/zori.py` accepts a `--geography` parameter as a free-form string but only `"county"` is implemented. Any other value hits an error at lines 310-315:

```python
if geography != "county":
    typer.echo(
        f"Error: Geography '{geography}' not yet supported. Only 'county' is implemented.",
        err=True,
    )
    raise typer.Exit(2)
```

This means a user can type `coclab aggregate zori --geography tract` and get a confusing runtime error instead of a validation error from the CLI framework.

## Recommended fix

Change the `geography` parameter from a free-form `str` to a constrained type. Two approaches:

**Option A (Restrict to county-only):** Change the Typer parameter type to a `Literal["county"]` or simply remove the `--geography` flag and hardcode `"county"`. Remove the dead guard clause. This is the cleanest option if no other geographies are planned.

**Option B (Use an enum):** Define a `ZoriGeography` enum with only `county` for now. This makes the CLI self-documenting about what's supported and produces a clean Typer error for invalid values.

## Files to change

- `coclab/cli/zori.py` — the `aggregate_zori` command function
  - The `geography` parameter declaration (find it via `grep -n "geography" coclab/cli/zori.py`)
  - The guard clause at lines 310-315
- Tests: `grep -r "geography" tests/test_cli*.py tests/test_zori*.py` to find affected tests

## Context

The ZORI aggregation itself (in `coclab/rents/aggregate.py`) works at the county level because ZORI source data from Zillow is county-level. Tract-level ZORI doesn't exist in the Zillow product. So "county" may be the only geography that will ever make sense here.

## Acceptance criteria

- `coclab aggregate zori --geography bogus` produces a clean Typer validation error (not a runtime error)
- OR the `--geography` flag is removed entirely if county is the only valid option
- `uv run --extra dev pytest` passes
close_reason: Constrained ZORI geography param to Literal[county] with Typer validation

title: Rename CLI command ingest acs → acs5-tract, remove deprecated aliases

title: Implement PEP population interpolation to January for PIT alignment
description:
PEP county population estimates use a July 1 reference date, but PIT counts are January point-in-time. The current code joins them on year with no temporal adjustment, creating a ~6-month mismatch.

### Proposed approach

Linearly interpolate between adjacent July 1 estimates to produce a January 1 estimate:

    pop_jan_2020 = 0.5 * pop_jul_2019 + 0.5 * pop_jul_2020

This is a simple average of the bracketing July estimates. For the first year in range (where no prior July exists), fall back to the raw July value.

### Data requirement: expanded PEP year range

Interpolating to January of year Y requires the July estimates from both Y-1 and Y. This means PEP ingestion must fetch one extra year before the panel start. For example, a 2015-2020 panel needs PEP data for 2014-2020 so that January 2015 can be interpolated from July 2014 and July 2015. The recipe executor (or preflight step) should detect when `align: point_in_time_jan` is set and automatically widen the PEP year range, or emit a clear error if the required bracketing year is missing.

### Where it fits

- The `align: point_in_time_jan` parameter already exists in the PEP dataset adapter (`coclab/recipe/default_dataset_adapters.py`) and is validated but not implemented.
- The interpolation should be triggered when `align: point_in_time_jan` is set in the recipe's PEP dataset params.
- Implementation likely belongs in `coclab/pep/` as a transform applied after ingest but before resampling, or within the recipe executor's dataset loading step.

### Context

The metro25-glynnfox recipe joins January PIT counts with July PEP populations for 2015-2020. The mismatch is small for slowly-changing populations but matters for fast-growing metros in the Glynn/Fox panel.
close_reason: Implemented interpolate_to_month temporal filter method. Added to recipe_schema.py, executor.py, PEP adapter validation, metro25-glynnfox recipe, and 5 unit tests.

title: Break circular imports among executor decomposition submodules
description: The executor decomposition landed in fdcdfe6 with five new modules (executor_transforms, executor_manifest, executor_inputs, executor_ct_alignment, executor_resample), but each module imports symbols from coclab.recipe.executor while executor.py eagerly re-imports symbols back from the partially initialized submodule. Fix by breaking the cycle (for example via a shared base/types module or by delaying/restructuring re-exports) and add a regression smoke test that imports each extracted submodule directly.

title: Add --type option to build-xwalks
description:
Add a `--type` option to `build-xwalks` to control which crosswalks are built.

## Current behavior
- Tract crosswalk is always built (required)
- County crosswalk is built if county files exist, otherwise skipped

## Proposed changes
Add `--type` option with values:
- `tracts` - build only tract crosswalk
- `counties` - build only county crosswalk
- `all` - build both (default, current behavior)

## Example usage
```bash
coclab build-xwalks --boundary 2025 --type counties --counties 2020
coclab build-xwalks --boundary 2025 --type tracts --tracts 2023
coclab build-xwalks --boundary 2025 --type all --tracts 2023 --counties 2020
```

## Notes
- When `--type counties`, the `--tracts` option should not be required
- When `--type tracts`, the `--counties` option should be ignored
close_reason: Implemented --type option with values 'tracts', 'counties', and 'all' (default)

title: aggregate_yearly_zori_to_metro renormalizes over metros with missing county ZORI rows
description:
Review follow-up from the 2026-03-28 coclab-i2fj audit.

`coclab/metro/zori.py:aggregate_yearly_zori_to_metro()` still silently fabricates metro rents when a member county has no yearly ZORI row at all.

Current code path:
- The function starts with `membership.merge(zori_yearly, on="county_fips", how="inner")`
- Any member county missing from `zori_yearly` disappears before weighting
- The remaining counties are then normalized to 100% of the metro, with no coverage/null guardrail

Direct repro:
```python
zori = pd.DataFrame({
    "county_fips": ["99001"],
    "year": [2020],
    "zori": [1000.0],
})
pop = pd.DataFrame({
    "county_fips": ["99001", "99002"],
    "year": [2020, 2020],
    "population": [100, 300],
})
membership = pd.DataFrame({
    "metro_id": ["M1", "M1"],
    "county_fips": ["99001", "99002"],
})
aggregate_yearly_zori_to_metro(zori, pop, county_membership_df=membership)
```
Observed result: returns `[{"metro_id": "M1", "year": 2020, "zori": 1000.0}]`, treating the single surviving county as full metro coverage.

Why this matters:
- The yearly helper used by `audit_panels._build_metro_source_panel()` can still fabricate metro ZORI values even after the missing-population fix.
- Missing county ZORI coverage is currently invisible in the yearly path.

Expected behavior:
- Preserve the full metro membership set before aggregation.
- Null out metro-year values when required county ZORI observations are missing, or emit an explicit coverage metric that downstream code enforces.
- Add a regression test for a metro-year with complete population weights but incomplete county ZORI coverage.
close_reason: Fixed: added incomplete county ZORI coverage guard in aggregate_yearly_zori_to_metro

title: Manifest drift risk: Parquet provenance vs sidecar JSON

These go through different code paths (_build_provenance returns a dict, _build_manifest returns a RecipeManifest) that duplicate the same asset deduplication and dataset/transform extraction logic. If one path is updated without the other, the two provenance records will silently diverge. Unify them: build the manifest once, embed its dict representation in Parquet metadata.

title: Include written artifact paths in coclab build recipe --json output
description:
The JSON success payload from `coclab build recipe --json` currently reports validation and pipeline step summaries, but it does not tell the caller exactly which panel artifact was written.

Affected code: `coclab/cli/recipe.py` around the success payload emitted after `execute_recipe(...)`.

Why this matters:
- Agent and CI callers need deterministic, machine-readable paths to the produced artifacts.
- Callers should not have to infer the output filename from recipe inputs or scrape logs.

Requested change:
Add an `artifacts` object to the success JSON with the concrete output paths, for example:

```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "recipe_name": "...",
  "artifacts": {
    "panel_path": "data/curated/panel/panel__metro__Y2016-2024@Dglynnfoxv1.parquet",
    "manifest_path": "data/curated/panel/panel__metro__Y2016-2024@Dglynnfoxv1.manifest.json",
    "diagnostics_path": "data/curated/panel/panel__metro__Y2016-2024@Dglynnfoxv1__diagnostics.json"
  }
}
```

Acceptance criteria:
- `coclab build recipe --json` includes the exact written artifact paths in its success payload.
- Paths are emitted for at least the panel parquet, manifest JSON, and diagnostics JSON when those artifacts are written.
- Tests cover the JSON contract so downstream automation can rely on it.

title: Implement or remove 'export' OutputKind in recipe executor
description:
## Problem

The recipe schema declares three output kinds at `coclab/recipe/recipe_schema.py:284`:

```python
OutputKind = Literal["panel", "diagnostics", "export"]
```

But the executor at `coclab/recipe/executor.py:2148-2170` only handles `"panel"` and `"diagnostics"`. If a recipe target declares `outputs: ["export"]`, the executor silently warns and produces nothing:

```python
unsupported = [
    o for o in declared_outputs if o not in ("panel", "diagnostics")
]
if unsupported:
    import warnings as _w
    _w.warn(
        f"Pipeline '{plan.pipeline_id}': target outputs "
        f"{unsupported} are declared but not yet implemented. "
        f"Only 'panel' and 'diagnostics' outputs are currently supported.",
        stacklevel=2,
    )
```

There is already a separate CLI command `coclab build recipe-export` (in `coclab/cli/recipe.py`) that handles export bundles. So the "export" OutputKind in the schema may be vestigial.

## Decision needed

**Option A (Remove from schema):** Change `OutputKind = Literal["panel", "diagnostics"]` and remove the warning block in the executor. This is the safer option if recipe-export is the intended export mechanism.

**Option B (Implement):** Add `_persist_export(plan, ctx)` to the executor alongside `_persist_outputs` and `_persist_diagnostics`. Study the existing `recipe-export` CLI command logic in `coclab/cli/recipe.py` to understand what "export" means in context.

## Files involved

- `coclab/recipe/recipe_schema.py:284` — `OutputKind` type definition
- `coclab/recipe/executor.py:2148-2170` — the warning block for unsupported outputs
- `coclab/cli/recipe.py` — the existing `recipe-export` CLI command (for understanding)

## Acceptance criteria

- No recipe can declare an output kind that silently does nothing
- Either "export" works end-to-end in the executor, or it's removed from the schema
- `uv run --extra dev pytest` passes
- Check that no committed recipe YAML files use `outputs: ["export"]`:
  `grep -r "export" recipes/`
close_reason: Removed vestigial export OutputKind from recipe schema and executor warning

title: Fix TIGER 2010 county download URL
description:
`coclab ingest-census --year 2010 --type counties` fails with 404.

## Current behavior
Code constructs URL as:
```
https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/TIGER2010/COUNTY/tl_2010_us_county.zip
```

## Actual URL
Census Bureau uses different structure for 2010:
```
https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/TIGER2010/COUNTY/2010/tl_2010_us_county10.zip
```

Two differences:
1. Extra `/2010/` subdirectory
2. Filename has `county10` suffix instead of `county`

## Fix
In `coclab/census/ingest/tiger_counties.py`, add special case for 2010:

```python
if year == 2010:
    url = f"{TIGER_BASE.format(year=year)}2010/tl_2010_us_county10.zip"
else:
    url = f"{TIGER_BASE.format(year=year)}tl_{year}_us_county.zip"
```
close_reason: Fixed in this session

title: Retain extra intersection fields in CoC↔County crosswalk
description:
Today, coclab/xwalks/county.py::build_coc_county_crosswalk returns only (coc_id, boundary_vintage, county_fips, area_share) where area_share is normalized by county area (intersection_area / county_area). This is sufficient for county→CoC aggregation (e.g., PEP/ZORI), but it discards the extra pieces needed to derive alternative normalizations (e.g., CoC→county via intersection_area / coc_area) or to recompute shares under different weighting schemes later.

Proposed change:
- Include at least intersection_area and both denominators (county_area, coc_area) in the saved county crosswalk parquet.
- Keep existing area_share semantics for backward compatibility, but make it possible to derive: county_share = intersection_area / county_area (current) and coc_share = intersection_area / coc_area (derivable).
- Consider also persisting county_vintage (and/or boundary_vintage) as explicit columns if helpful for provenance consistency.

Touch points:
- coclab/xwalks/county.py (compute/retain areas; update output schema)
- Downstream consumers of xwalk__B{boundary}xC{counties}.parquet (PEP/ZORI aggregation) to ensure they ignore extra columns and preserve behavior
- Manual updates: manual-obsidian/06-Data-Model.md (county crosswalk schema), manual-obsidian/04-CLI-Reference.md (build xwalks output description)
- Add/adjust tests for the county crosswalk schema and invariants (shares within bounds, reproducible denominators).
acceptance_criteria:
1) County crosswalk parquet includes intersection_area, county_area, and coc_area columns (plus existing columns).
2) Existing pipelines that read county crosswalks continue to work unchanged (extra columns tolerated).
3) Documentation updated to reflect the richer county crosswalk schema and how to derive either normalization.
4) Tests cover basic invariants (areas > 0, intersection_area <= denom areas, derived shares in [0,1], etc.).
close_reason: Implemented: added intersection_area, county_area, coc_area columns to county crosswalk; added tests; updated docs

title: Make recipe preflight the preferred build entry path
description: Shift recipe builds to a preflight-first workflow. For any recipe, the system should validate structure, resolve the execution plan, inspect all required datasets, transforms, and prerequisite artifacts by year and geometry, report blocking gaps in one pass, and emit a machine-readable data gaps manifest with actionable remediation hints. The coclab build recipe command should use this preflight as its default entry path before execution. The coclab build recipe-preflight command should provide the same analysis as a standalone command for CI, agents, and manual review. The implementation should consolidate currently scattered checks across cli/recipe.py, planner.py, executor.py, status.py, and list_artifacts.py into a recipe-scoped readiness workflow.
design: Parallel implementation lanes: (1) build a reusable preflight analyzer and report model over resolved execution plans; (2) extract non-executing dataset and transform probes from the executor so schema, year, filter, and static-broadcast checks can run before execution; (3) add structured data gaps and remediation metadata, including suggested commands and affected years/geometries; (4) surface the analyzer through recipe-preflight and then route build recipe through it as the preferred path. Keep report generation separate from CLI rendering so multiple front ends can consume the same core logic.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A standalone preflight command exists for recipes and returns blocking gaps in one pass with JSON support. 2. Preflight resolves recipe plans per pipeline and inspects required datasets, transforms, columns, years, and prerequisite artifacts before execution. 3. Preflight can emit a structured data gaps manifest suitable for manual review or follow-on automation. 4. The build recipe command runs the same preflight logic before execution and refuses to execute when blocking gaps remain. 5. Tests cover both human-readable and JSON outputs, representative missing-artifact cases, and multi-year recipe edge cases.
notes: Reopened on 2026-03-26 after regression review. Current HEAD still has unresolved gaps in the preflight implementation: file_set checks are not recipe-scoped, weighted-transform support datasets are not preflighted, build recipe can bypass shared preflight output through legacy validation, and --gaps omits uncovered-year blockers. Child tasks coclab-pu6j.1, coclab-pu6j.2, coclab-pu6j.3, and coclab-hh6d have been reopened with concrete repro details.
close_reason: All child tasks completed. Recipe preflight is now the preferred build entry path: plan-scoped path checking, shared support-dataset probes, gaps manifest with uncovered-year blockers, and build recipe consistently routes through preflight. 1881 tests passing.

title: Build reusable recipe preflight analyzer over resolved execution plans
description: Create the reusable core that powers recipe-scoped readiness analysis. This task owns the non-CLI preflight engine: resolve each pipeline plan, enumerate required dataset-year inputs and transforms, aggregate findings across pipelines, and return a structured report object that CLI commands can render or serialize. Keep the report schema separate from presentation so the same analyzer can power recipe-preflight, build recipe prechecks, tests, and later automation hooks. Scope should stay centered on recipe planning and report assembly rather than dataset-schema probing details.
design: Own the report model and orchestration layer, not CLI formatting. The analyzer should consume a parsed recipe and resolved plans, deduplicate repeated dataset-year requirements across pipelines where sensible, and produce a stable report structure that downstream tasks can enrich with schema probes and remediation hints.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is a reusable preflight entrypoint outside the CLI layer. 2. It resolves each pipeline plan and returns a structured report covering required inputs, transforms, years, and findings. 3. The report is stable enough for both human CLI rendering and JSON serialization. 4. Tests cover multi-pipeline recipes, repeated dataset-year reuse, and planner failure propagation.
notes: Reopened on 2026-03-26 after review of current HEAD. Verified regression: preflight path checks are not recipe-scoped. Repro: a recipe with universe=2020-2020 and a file_set segment covering 2020-2022, with only data/pit_2020.parquet present, still returns blocked due to missing 2021/2022 files. Current behavior comes from _check_dataset_paths iterating every year in every file_set segment instead of only required dataset-years resolved from the recipe plan. Acceptance is not met until preflight enumerates only recipe-required dataset-year inputs.
close_reason: Preflight analyzer is now recipe-scoped: _check_dataset_paths uses resolved execution plan tasks instead of iterating all segment years. Plans are resolved before path checks. Deduplicates repeated dataset-year checks across pipelines. Planner errors for uncovered years now surface in the gaps manifest as UNCOVERED_YEARS findings.

title: Extract non-executing dataset and transform probes from the recipe executor
description: Refactor runtime validation logic out of recipe execution so preflight can inspect datasets and transforms without running the build. This task should carve reusable helpers for year-column detection, geo-column detection, required-measure checks, temporal-filter validation, static-broadcast detection, support-dataset checks for weighted transforms, and transform prerequisite probing. Update the executor to consume the shared helpers so preflight and execution stay behaviorally aligned instead of forking two validation code paths.
design: This task should create shared, non-executing inspection helpers with disjoint ownership from the higher-level preflight report assembly. The goal is to reuse executor semantics without copying code: preflight and runtime execution should call the same validation primitives for dataset schemas, filters, measure presence, and transform prerequisites.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Shared helpers exist for dataset schema inspection and transform prerequisite checks without executing a build. 2. Executor code is refactored to consume those helpers. 3. Preflight can detect missing or ambiguous year and geo columns, missing measures, temporal-filter issues, and unsafe static broadcast conditions before execution. 4. Tests verify shared behavior from both the helper layer and executor call sites.
notes: Reopened on 2026-03-26 after review of current HEAD. Verified regression: preflight still misses support-dataset checks for weighted transforms. Repro: a transform with weighting.scheme=population, population_source=weights, population_field=total_population returns recipe-preflight --json ready=true even when data/weights.parquet lacks total_population; build recipe then fails at execution with missing population field. Acceptance is not met until preflight and runtime share support-dataset validation for weighted transforms.
close_reason: Extracted shared get_weighted_transform_requirements() helper in probes.py. Added probe_support_dataset() for validating population_source field presence without loading data. Executor refactored to consume shared helper. Preflight now detects missing support-dataset fields before execution via new _check_support_datasets() stage and MISSING_SUPPORT_DATASET finding kind.

title: Route build recipe through preflight and update recipe entrypoint docs
description: Once the preflight engine, probes, and gaps manifest are stable, make preflight the preferred user path. Update the build recipe command to invoke the same preflight logic before execution, print a concise readiness summary, fail fast on blocking gaps, and point users to the emitted gaps artifact when relevant. Refresh help text, command examples, and recipe-facing docs so agents and humans are nudged toward preflight-first usage rather than iterative fail-and-retry execution.
design: Treat this as the final integration lane after the analyzer, command surface, and gaps contract are stable. The command should not reimplement preflight; it should invoke the same shared engine, surface a concise readiness summary, and steer users toward the standalone preflight command and gaps artifact when execution is blocked.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The build recipe command invokes shared preflight logic before execution. 2. Blocking preflight gaps prevent execution and surface clear next steps. 3. Help text, examples, and recipe-facing docs present preflight as the normal first step. 4. Tests cover successful execution after clean preflight and refusal to execute when blocking gaps remain.
notes: Reopened on 2026-03-26 after review of current HEAD. Verified regression: build recipe still exits through legacy validation before the shared preflight path for some missing-dataset cases. Repro: a recipe with path=data/missing.parquet returns build recipe --json status=error with validation.errors instead of status=blocked with preflight payload. Acceptance is not met until build recipe consistently routes blocking readiness failures through shared preflight output.
close_reason: Build recipe now routes all dataset path failures through shared preflight output (status=blocked with preflight payload) instead of legacy validation.errors. CLI _validate_recipe no longer performs dataset-path checking. Preflight warnings are printed during build recipe. Docstrings updated to present preflight as the recommended first step.

title: Document Connecticut county special-case handling in manual-obsidian
description:
Add a clear note in the manual under `manual-obsidian/` describing how CoC-Lab handles the Connecticut county-equivalent transition.

Goal:
Make the special-case behavior explicit for both human users and agents.

Content to cover:
- Connecticut switched from 8 legacy counties to 9 planning regions as Census county-equivalent geography.
- Why this affects county-native recipe inputs such as PEP and ZORI.
- What the near-term CoC-Lab policy is: special-case normalization and/or explicit exclusion/failure guardrail rather than silent corruption.
- Which outputs or recipe situations are affected.
- How users should interpret build diagnostics when the CT special case is applied.

Notes:
- Link the manual note to the relevant runtime behavior and bead IDs so future sessions can see both the rationale and the implementation status.
- The documentation should make it obvious that this is intentional handling, not an unexplained data quirk.
close_reason: Completed

title: vintage_sets helpers are dead code in recipe planning
description: planner.py defines expand_vintage_set() and resolve_vintage_tuple(), and recipe_schema.py exposes top-level vintage_sets, but _resolve_dataset_year() and _resolve_via_file_set() never consult them. The only callers are tests and the package re-export. That means review/test beads that assume file_set planning uses vintage_sets are pointing at a nonexistent integration path. Either wire vintage_sets into planner/file_set resolution or remove the dead API surface.
close_reason: Removed dead expand_vintage_set() and resolve_vintage_tuple() from planner; removed from __init__.py exports and tests. Schema types retained for future use.

title: LAUS ingest silently writes partial-measure parquet when one core measure is missing
description:
Review follow-up from coclab-7isb.

The current LAUS ingest only fails when all four measures are null for a metro. If BLS returns three measures but drops one core series (for example unemployment_rate for all metros), ingest_laus_metro still writes a parquet with nulls in a required measure column instead of failing fast.

Evidence:
- coclab/ingest/bls_laus.py checks only df[measure_cols].isna().all(axis=1) before writing.
- Reproduction in review: patch fetch_laus_annual_averages to omit all unemployment_rate series; ingest succeeds and writes 25 null unemployment_rate values.

Expected:
- Treat any missing required LAUS measure for any metro as an ingest failure, or explicitly justify a narrower contract.
- Add a regression test covering partial-measure loss, not just all-null metros / partially missing metros.

Acceptance:
- ingest_laus_metro fails fast when any of the four required LAUS measures is missing for any metro.
- tests/test_laus.py covers the partial-measure-loss case.
notes:
Reopened after review of the latest local patch. The new guard only fails when a required measure is null for every metro. It still writes a parquet when one required measure is missing for a subset of metros.

Reproduction:
- Patch fetch_laus_annual_averages to omit unemployment_rate for GF01-GF05 only while returning all other LAUS series.
- ingest_laus_metro succeeds and writes a parquet with 5 null unemployment_rate rows instead of failing.

Acceptance is not met until any missing required LAUS measure for any metro causes ingest failure, with regression coverage for the subset-of-metros case.
close_reason: done

title: Clean up measures/acs.py — remove duplicate fetch code
close_reason: Closed

title: Retire named-build workflow commands subsumed by recipe system
description: Remove 6 commands that form the named-build workflow, now fully subsumed by the recipe system: build panel, build create, build list, build export, generate catalog, list artifacts. Also remove supporting modules (coclab/builds.py helpers, coclab/panel/builder, coclab/export/) that are only used by these commands. The aggregate commands survive — they produce curated inputs that recipes consume.
notes: Reviewed current HEAD after child closures. I agree with the core runtime migration: aggregate/build_xwalks/zori no longer send users to deleted build-create scaffolding, and optional --build plus explicit --years is a coherent replacement. I do not agree that the migration is fully complete, because the docs/help and regression-coverage child tasks were closed prematurely. Outstanding drift verified on current HEAD: coclab/cli/main.py still advertises retired 'artifacts' and 'catalogs' concepts in group help, and manual-obsidian workflow examples still invoke aggregate commands without the now-required --years/--build contract. Reopened qgpk.3 and qgpk.4 to track those remaining gaps precisely.
close_reason: All child tasks completed. Help strings, docs, and regression tests updated for recipe-first CLI migration.

comments.author: mlm
comments.text: Reopened: Review of 24b5a09 found remaining docs/help and regression-coverage gaps; closure was premature.

title: Migrate surviving aggregate/generate commands off deleted build-create workflow
description: Several surviving commands still require named-build state and still point users at the deleted 'coclab build create' command. Complete the migration by removing that dependency or replacing it with a supported recipe-first equivalent. Scope includes aggregate.py build validation/output resolution, build_xwalks.py build-local output pathing, zori.py build-local output/xwalk resolution, and any shared helpers they rely on.
design: Treat recipe-first as the source of truth. Prefer one of two clean end states: (a) surviving commands operate directly on curated inputs or explicit output paths without named-build manifests, or (b) they are explicitly retired and replaced by recipe-native flows. Do not leave hybrid paths that still require builds/<name> state while the scaffold command is gone.
acceptance_criteria: 1. No surviving CLI command instructs users to run 'coclab build create'. 2. aggregate/build_xwalks/zori paths that currently require --build either work without named-build scaffolding or are replaced/retired with clear guidance to recipe-native commands. 3. Error messages tell the user what to do next using only supported commands. 4. Tests cover at least one missing-input path for each migrated command family and verify the new guidance.
close_reason: Made --build optional on all aggregate commands; --years required when --build omitted; removed all 'coclab build create' references from error messages

title: Remove remaining coclab.builds runtime dependencies after CLI migration
description: After the command-surface migration, remove or isolate the remaining named-build runtime layer. Audit coclab.builds.py consumers including status and helper modules, decide which legacy behaviors are still intentionally supported, and delete or narrow code that only exists to serve the retired named-build workflow.
design: This task should happen after the command-surface migration is clear. The goal is not to break useful status/reporting features, but to stop carrying build-manifest logic as a hidden substrate for a supposedly retired workflow. If a small compatibility shim remains, document it explicitly instead of leaving implicit dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Remaining imports of coclab.builds are intentional and documented, or removed. 2. status/help text no longer implies named builds are the primary workflow unless that support is intentionally retained. 3. Dead code and helpers that only served deleted commands are removed. 4. Tests cover any intentionally retained legacy compatibility behavior.
close_reason: Removed dead code from coclab/builds.py (ensure_build_dir, populate_base_assets, write_build_manifest, catalog functions). Cleaned up test_build_manifest.py.

title: Finish docs/help cleanup for recipe-first CLI migration
description: The command pruning removed build create/list/export and list artifacts, but docs and help text are still inconsistent. Clean up README, AGENTS.md guidance, Typer group help strings, manual-obsidian references, and module reference pages so they describe only supported workflows and modules.
design: Prefer concise recipe-first guidance. If aggregate or status commands still mention builds, make that relationship explicit and accurate. Keep operator guidance aligned with the actual registered commands and current architecture.
acceptance_criteria: 1. No user-facing docs or help text refer users to deleted commands. 2. Module-reference docs no longer list deleted modules as active. 3. README quick-start and CLI highlights are internally consistent about recipe-first versus any intentionally retained legacy paths. 4. Tests or doc checks cover the most user-visible command/help surfaces where practical.
notes: Reopened after reviewing commit 24b5a09. The runtime migration is mostly correct, but docs/help cleanup is incomplete. Current repo state still has operator-facing drift: coclab/cli/main.py help says 'list available datasets and artifacts' and 'Generate crosswalks, metro definitions, and catalogs' even though list artifacts and generate catalog were retired; manual-obsidian/09-Workflows.md still shows aggregate commands without required --years or --build. Reproduced on current HEAD: 'uv run --extra dev python -m coclab.cli.main aggregate pit' and '... aggregate pep' exit with 'Error: --years is required when --build is not specified.'; '... aggregate zori --align pit_january' does the same. manual-obsidian/01-Overview.md also still frames named builds as the execution style and aggregate outputs as occurring inside named builds, which is no longer the recipe-first default described by this migration. Acceptance is not met until help strings and workflow docs reflect the actual command contract.
close_reason: Fixed stale help strings (artifacts, catalogs) in main.py, updated aggregate examples in 09-Workflows.md with required --years, updated 01-Overview.md to reflect recipe-first workflow, fixed retired command references in devdocs.

comments.author: mlm
comments.text: Reopened: Review of 24b5a09 found remaining docs/help and regression-coverage gaps; closure was premature.

title: Add migration regression coverage for removed named-build commands and stale guidance
description: Add regression tests that lock the migration in place. Cover top-level help output, subgroup help output, and representative error paths so the CLI cannot regress back to advertising deleted commands or stale build-create guidance.
design: Test the highest-signal surfaces rather than every string in the repo: main help, build/list/generate help, aggregate missing-build or missing-input errors, and any docs-backed smoke tests that already exist. The goal is to catch real operator-facing regressions without making the suite brittle.
acceptance_criteria: 1. CLI tests fail if deleted commands reappear in help output. 2. CLI tests fail if representative error paths suggest 'coclab build create' after the migration is finished. 3. At least one test covers a representative recipe-first replacement path or guidance string. 4. The new coverage runs in the normal test suite without special external data.
notes: Reopened after reviewing commit 24b5a09. The added regression tests cover command presence and stale 'coclab build create' guidance, but they do not cover the remaining user-visible drift that still exists on current HEAD. In particular, tests do not fail on stale Typer group descriptions in coclab/cli/main.py ('artifacts', 'catalogs'), and they do not smoke-test the documented aggregate workflow examples that now require --years when --build is omitted. Current targeted suite still passes ('uv run --extra dev pytest tests/test_cli.py tests/test_aggregate_cli.py -q' => 53 passed), which confirms the gap: the suite misses the broken docs/help contract rather than proving it is fixed. Acceptance is not met until regression coverage reaches those surfaces.
close_reason: Added 3 regression tests covering group help descriptions for stale terminology (list/artifacts, generate/catalogs, main/both). All 9 regression tests pass.

title: Add quota-resistant fallback for LAUS metro backfills
description: While updating recipes/metro25-glynnfox-laus.yaml to cover 2015-2023, the backfill command 'uv run python -m coclab.cli.main ingest laus-metro --start-year 2015 --end-year 2023 --json' failed for every year because the BLS LAUS API daily threshold was already exhausted for anonymous access. The recipe now plans correctly, but end-to-end verification is blocked unless the missing annual files can be produced without depending on a fresh API quota window. Add a fallback path for metro LAUS ingest/backfill, ideally using an official BLS bulk/flat-file source or another lower-request-count strategy. If a BLS quota is hit, the program should explicitly tell the user to either supply a BLS API key or wait until the quota resets, and add regression coverage for quota-exhausted conditions.
acceptance_criteria: When LAUS ingest/backfill hits a BLS quota or daily-threshold condition, the CLI returns an actionable error telling the user to either supply a BLS API key or wait until the quota resets, and regression coverage exercises the quota-exhausted path.
close_reason: Added BlsQuotaExhausted with actionable hint covering --api-key/BLS_API_KEY registration and threshold reset; CLI surfaces hint in text + JSON, short-circuits backfills, and reports reason=bls_quota_exhausted. Regression coverage: TestBlsQuotaExhausted (4 quota response shapes + has-key vs anon hint + non-quota fall-through) and 4 CLI tests for single-year/json/backfill/partial-success.

title: Manual xwalk instructions conflict with build-measures xwalk requirements
description: Manual says build-xwalks with tracts 2023 for all boundaries. build-measures for 2015-2023 then fails looking for xwalks like coc_tract_xwalk__2015__2014.parquet (ACS vintage year) and errors: Run 'coclab build-xwalks --boundary 2015 --tracts 2014' first. This makes the Typical Use Sequence non-runnable as written.
notes: Re-tested after manual/CLI update: build-measures still expects xwalks like coc_tract_xwalk__2015__2014.parquet; xwalks built per manual with tracts 2023 don’t satisfy build-measures for 2015-2023.
close_reason: Added --tracts 2023 to all build-measures commands in manual to match the crosswalks built in Phase 2

title: Decouple asset store, outputs, and export destinations via configurable storage roots
description: Implement the storage roots plan in devdocs/storage-roots-configuration-plan.md. Separate CoC-Lab internal asset storage from downstream-consumable outputs, keep export as an explicit replication workflow, and make root placement configurable via CLI/env/config.
close_reason: All 5 sub-tasks completed. Storage roots feature fully implemented: config loading with 5-layer precedence, path helpers, all asset/output paths routed through helpers, root-aware manifests, export with --destination, 54 regression tests.

title: Add runtime config loading and precedence for storage roots
description: Introduce config loading for asset_store_root and output_root with precedence CLI > env > repo coclab.yaml > user config > defaults. Add a central config/path layer rather than scattered Path("data/...") defaults.
close_reason: Implemented coclab/config.py (StorageConfig + load_config with 5-layer precedence) and coclab/paths.py (asset_store_root, raw_root, curated_root, curated_dir, output_root, output_dir helpers). Added pyyaml dependency. 41 new tests covering all precedence layers and edge cases.

title: Route recipe-built outputs and sidecars through output_root
description: Refactor recipe execution so panel outputs, diagnostics, and manifest sidecars resolve from output_root rather than the curated asset store. Preserve canonical filenames while separating placement from naming.
close_reason: Threaded StorageConfig through execute_recipe → _execute_plan → ExecutionContext → _resolve_panel_output_file. Panel outputs, manifests, and diagnostics now resolve from config.output_root. Added --output-root and --asset-store-root CLI flags to recipe command. Handles outputs outside project root gracefully.

title: Route internal asset reads and writes through asset_store_root
description: Refactor ingest, aggregate, crosswalk, registry, and discovery code to resolve raw and curated internal assets from asset_store_root via central helpers instead of hardcoded repo-relative data paths.
close_reason: Replaced hardcoded Path("data/...") defaults across 40+ files with centralized path helpers from coclab/paths.py. All ingest, aggregate, crosswalk, registry, and discovery code now resolves paths through configurable storage roots. Updated 12 tests for absolute path resolution.

title: Make recipe manifests and export root-aware; rename recipe-export --output to --destination
description: Update manifest records to identify logical roots (asset_store vs output), teach export resolution to use configured roots, and rename the recipe-export destination flag from --output to --destination while keeping export as a distinct replication workflow.
close_reason: Added logical root metadata to AssetRecord (root field: asset_store, output, or None for legacy). Updated export_bundle to resolve paths via StorageConfig roots. Renamed recipe-export --output to --destination. Updated executor to classify consumed assets by root. Backward compatible with legacy manifests.

title: Add docs and regression coverage for configurable storage roots
description: Add tests for config precedence, default compatibility, separated asset/output roots, and export destination behavior. Update README and manual storage model language to reflect asset store vs outputs vs export.
close_reason: Added 13 regression tests in test_storage_roots.py covering root-aware export, legacy backward compat, manifest roundtrips, classify_path, and config integration. Combined with 41 tests from test_config.py/test_paths.py, total coverage is 54 tests for storage roots.

title: Guard PEP aggregation against empty year filters after vintage discovery
description: Follow-up review on commit 6274905. load_pep_county now discovers vintage files correctly, but aggregate_pep_to_coc still crashes when the requested year window is absent from the loaded data. Repro: create a pep_county__v2024.parquet with only year 2024, then call aggregate_pep_to_coc(..., pep_path=v2024, start_year=2018, end_year=2018). Current result is ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to integer at actual_start/actual_end derivation. Expected: raise a clear actionable error describing that no PEP rows remain after applying the requested year filter. Add regression coverage for this path.
close_reason: Added explicit empty-year-filter guard in aggregate_pep_to_coc with regression coverage

title: RecipeCache stores absolute path as FileIdentity.path
description: cache.py:75 stores the absolute path string in FileIdentity.path, but manifest.py AssetRecord.path expects project-relative paths. The executor correctly uses relative_to() when creating AssetRecords (executor.py:1003), but if anyone consumes FileIdentity.path directly for provenance, they'd get absolute paths that break portability. Consider making FileIdentity store relative paths or documenting the contract.
close_reason: Documented FileIdentity.path contract: path may be absolute, consumers needing relative paths should use relative_to() on original Path. No code reads this field.

title: Add shared raw snapshot utilities for API ingests
description: Introduce reusable helpers for writing canonical API raw snapshots under data/raw/<source_type>/..., including request metadata, paginated payload capture, deterministic serialization, and hash/size helpers. Use this utility in downstream migration tasks.
close_reason: Closed

title: Align PEP output scoping: avoid writing coc_pep aggregates to global data/curated/pep by default
description: Current behavior allows coclab build pep to write CoC-level aggregate files (e.g., coc_pep__B2024xC2024__warea_share__2010_2024.parquet) into global data/curated/pep when --build is not provided. This is inconsistent with build-scoped aggregate flow and can mix reusable inputs with scenario/build outputs.
acceptance_criteria: Decide and implement canonical behavior: either require --build for CoC-level PEP aggregates or route default outputs to a clearly build-scoped location; update CLI help/docs and tests to reflect expected location; add migration note for existing global coc_pep__* files.
close_reason: Removed orphaned build_pep() function from pep.py; aggregate pep already requires --build and outputs to build-scoped directory

title: Agent C: ZORI eligibility rules and provenance
description: Define ZORI-eligible analysis universe: coverage_ratio >= zori_min_coverage (default 0.90). Add zori_is_eligible boolean column. For ineligible rows: set zori_coc=null, rent_to_income=null. Add provenance fields: rent_metric, rent_alignment, zori_min_coverage. High dominance = warning only, not exclusion. See background/coclab_rent_to_income_agent_instructions.md.

title: Fix coclab status --json PIT asset counts when curated PIT files exist
